conversation.text: I just finished the quiz and read through my assessment. I clicked to chat with you to learn more.

Hi Jamie! Great to hear from you.

One thing I want to dig into is the non-restorative sleep piece, because with your pregnancy goals, that's where a lot of the real impact lies. Your body is showing multiple signs of nighttime stress activation (racing heart, sweating, frequent urination, nightmares), and the fact that you still don't feel rested even with enough hours suggests the *quality* of sleep is being undermined at a deeper level.

Here's what's interesting: your morning pattern of skipping breakfast and running on coffee actually sets the stage for exactly this kind of nighttime disruption. When the body spends the first half of the day in a stress-hormone-driven state to maintain blood sugar, those systems don't just shut off at bedtime. They carry forward.

I'm curious about your digestive issues specifically. What kinds of symptoms are you experiencing, and do you notice them getting worse at particular times (after meals, at night, etc.)?

yeah most likely after dinner.
what do you mean skipping breakfast and running on coffee is a disruption? i stop caffeine before noon

Good that you cut caffeine early. The issue isn't actually the caffeine itself, it's the *fasting*. When you skip breakfast, your body has to manufacture its own blood sugar using cortisol and adrenaline. Coffee amplifies this further by stimulating those same stress hormones. So even though you feel fine in the morning (those hormones are energizing), your body is running on stress rather than food.

This matters because chronically elevated morning stress hormones have downstream effects that show up later, including at night. They promote gut inflammation, suppress digestive function, and keep the nervous system in a more activated state overall.

Now, your digestive symptoms worsening after dinner is a useful clue. That timing suggests the issue is likely in the small intestine or beyond, where food arrives 1-3 hours after eating. Bacterial overgrowth in that region can produce gas, bloating, and discomfort as microbes ferment the meal. It can also trigger serotonin release from the gut lining, which would directly feed into your nighttime sleep disruption.

What specifically are you feeling after dinner? Bloating, pain, nausea, something else?
what do you mean? fasting is great so many things!

I get why you'd think that, it's one of the most popular ideas in health right now. And fasting does have some real short-term benefits, but here's the catch: many of those benefits come *from* the stress response itself. Cortisol and adrenaline are anti-inflammatory in the short term, they mobilize energy, sharpen focus, and can even suppress gut symptoms temporarily. So people feel great initially.

But the mechanism that makes fasting "work" is the same one that's likely driving your sleep problems. Your body is producing stress hormones to compensate for the lack of incoming fuel. Over time, that chronic activation wears on the system, particularly the gut, thyroid, and sleep architecture.

This is actually something we see a lot at Prism. People adopt fasting or low-carb approaches, feel better for a while because they've accidentally lowered the stress response's *symptoms*, but the underlying stress load keeps building. Then seemingly unrelated things start showing up: poor sleep, cold hands, digestive issues.

Sound familiar?

What are those post-dinner symptoms like for you specifically? That'll help me connect more of the dots.

Yes. A [systematic review and meta-analysis](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.3109/10253890.2015.1121984) across 13 studies found that caloric restriction significantly elevates serum cortisol, with fasting showing a "very strong effect." And research from the Mayo Clinic found that [fasting induced a 1.8-fold increase in 24-hour cortisol production](https://mayoclinic.elsevierpure.com/en/publications/fasting-as-a-metabolic-stress-paradigm-selectively-amplifies-cort) and actually delayed peak cortisol to the afternoon, disrupting the normal daily rhythm.

This doesn't mean fasting can't have some benefits in certain contexts. But for someone with your symptom profile, where stress physiology is clearly already overactivated at night, adding a daily fast on top of that is likely compounding the problem rather than helping it.

So back to the piece that'll help me connect things further: what are those post-dinner digestive symptoms like?
